summary: Joel provides a casual update on Fog Creek Software's early days, where the team is simultaneously renovating their office space and preparing to ship FogBUGZ 2.0. He contrasts their scrappy, bootstrap approach with venture-capital funded startups. He outlines their beta testing philosophy—limiting testers to committed installers and providing real-time phone support—as a direct counter to Netscape's approach of ignoring beta feedback. He also teases an upcoming unannounced product.
key_points: Fog Creek's bootstrap mentality meant founders personally renovated their office, contrasting with VC-funded competitors
FogBUGZ 2.0 beta testing strategy involves selecting committed testers and providing dedicated real-time support during installation
Joel emphasizes listening to customers obsessively, even soliciting feedback from people who tried the demo but didn't buy
Free upgrades for existing 1.0 customers signals a customer-loyalty-first pricing approach
money_quotes: You venture-capital funded wimps probably never had to touch spackle.
I slammed Netscape for using the entire world as beta-testers, and then ignoring the bug reports that came in.
We're inviting a select group of people to install the beta, and asking them to tell us when they will be installing it.
During that time, we will literally be standing by the phone on our toll-free line to help them with any problems that come up.
I'm obsessive about listening to customers.
key_insight: A bootstrap startup's competitive advantage lies in doing things that don't scale—like personally supporting every beta tester—which VC-funded companies are too removed to bother with.
